chunk_summary: Chronic nonbacterial osteomyelitis (CNO) is an autoinflammatory bone disorder in children, requiring differentiation from infection or malignancy. Diagnosis involves imaging, biopsy, and exclusion of bacterial causes, with NSAIDs as first-line therapy.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and typical sites of chronic nonbacterial osteomyelitis (CNO).
Learn diagnostic strategies, including imaging and biopsy, to differentiate CNO from infection or malignancy.
Identify first-line and advanced treatment options for CNO, including NSAIDs and biologic therapies.
Recognize the importance of long-term follow-up in managing CNO complications.
key_facts: CNO commonly affects school-age children and adolescents, with typical sites including the clavicle, vertebrae, pelvis, and long bone metaphyses.
Whole-body MRI is the imaging modality of choice to identify multifocal inflammatory patterns and clinically silent lesions.
Histology typically shows sterile chronic inflammation, not pus-producing infection.
First-line treatment is with NSAIDs, with corticosteroids, bisphosphonates, or biologics reserved for refractory cases.
Vertebral involvement can lead to compression deformities, necessitating long-term follow-up.
CNO diagnosis should be suspected in multifocal, recurrent, or antibiotic-unresponsive osteomyelitis cases.
reference_quote: "Whole-body magnetic resonance imaging is the imaging study of choice because it identifies clinically silent lesions and helps distinguish a multifocal inflammatory pattern from isolated pyogenic disease." (Source: Chronic Nonbacterial Osteomyelitis in Children — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8303960/)
